content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 12, 2017

Michael Calabrese
Chief Counsel
San Bernandino CERA
348 W. Hospitality Lane
San Bernardino, CA 92415

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-087

Dear Mr. Calabrese:

You are the Chief Counsel of the San Bernardino County Employees' Retirement
Association (SBCERA) and this letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we do not advise on
any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are
also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Additionally, the Commission
does not advise about past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).) Therefore, nothing in this advice
letter should be construed to advise about any conduct that has already taken place.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Bernardino
County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive
a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you
that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Do SBCERA staff have a conflict of interest in participating in SBCERA Board decisions
related to eliminating a pick-up benefit in their employment contracts either in their official or
individual capacities?

CONCLUSION

Yes, SBCERA staff that receive the pick-up benefit have a conflict of interest in
participating in SBCERA Board decisions to eliminate the benefit in their official capacities under
Section 1090, though the rule of necessity may allow limited participation in some circumstances.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 2

However, the employees are not prohibited from participating in their individual capacities as explained below.

FACTS

SBCERA is a retirement system formed under the County Employees' Retirement Law of 1937. You are the Chief Counsel of SBCERA and are writing for advice on your own behalf and for several dozen similarly situated employees of SBCERA.

Many of SBCERA's employees currently receive, as part of their compensation, a "pick-up" by SBCERA of the retirement contributions that the employees would otherwise be required to make toward the funding of their pensions. This pick-up is up to seven percent of an employee's compensation earnable. This benefit is limited to "Tier 1" employees, which are employees not subject to the Public Employees' Pension Reform Act of 2012. Approximately two-thirds of SBCERA's employees either receive this benefit now, or are eligible to receive it in the future if they remain employed. This pick-up benefit is set forth in SBCERA's generally applicable "Employment Resolution." The Employment Resolution is incorporated by reference into each employee's contract of employment. All SBCERA employees have individual written employment contracts with SBCERA; none are represented by a union, so there are no collectively bargained memorandums of understanding.

At a recent public meeting of SBCERA's Board of Retirement (Board) the Board's Vice-Chair stated that she wished a future agenda to include a proposal to eliminate the pick-up benefit for SBCERA employees. Shortly after this public meeting the Board Chair visited both SBCERA's Chief Executive Officer and yourself to inform you that he intended to move forward with placing this item on an upcoming agenda, although he did not know exactly when. Upon conducting independent research, and conferring with outside counsel, you concluded the consideration of such an item would involve a substantive amendment to the terms of the employment contract of each employee who receives, or may in the future become eligible for the pick-up benefit.

No member of the SBCERA Board has any potential conflict in this decision, as Board Members are not SBCERA employees. However, employees of SBCERA including yourself may be asked to provide staff assistance on this matter such as providing input on the pick-up issue.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers.

Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103 and the financial interests implicated by your facts are the following:

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 3

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) "Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

The Act excludes from the definition of "income" any "salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem, and social security, or other similar benefit payments received from a state, local, or federal government agency . . . ." (Section 82030(b)(2).) The salary paid by a state, local or Federal government agency are generally not considered "income" under Section 82030(b)(2) and Regulation 18232(a). Therefore, the SBCERA employees will not have an interest in the Board as a source of income. However, material financial effects on an official's government salary may still be disqualifying under limited circumstances as a material and foreseeable financial effect on the official's personal finances that is distinct from other officials. (See Boydston Advice Letter, No. A-12-133.) Thus, we continue the analysis based on financial effects to SBCERA employees' personal finances.

Foreseeability

A public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision, if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

Regulation 18701 provides in pertinent part:

"(a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) - (6)."

SBCERA employees may be asked to advise the Board on upcoming decisions involving the elimination of a pick-up that is a supplement to SBCERA employees' income. Therefore, SBCERA employee income is the subject of the decision before the SBCERA Board. Therefore, SBCERA employees' personal finances are the subject of this governmental decision.

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 4

Materiality

Regulation 18702.5 provides the appropriate materiality standard for personal financial
effects:

"(a) A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate
family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate
family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the
decision.

(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a), a personal financial effect does not
include:

(1) Any establishment of or change to benefits provided under an employment
or retirement policy for employees or retirees if the financial effect of the decision
applies equally to all employees in the same bargaining unit or other
representative group. . . ."

SBCERA's Tier 1 employees will receive a measurable loss if the pick-up is eliminated.
However, this loss would apply equally to all employees who currently receive or are entitled to
receive the pick-up in the future per their employment contracts, therefore the financial effect upon
these employees' personal finances is not material pursuant to Regulation 18702.5(b)(1). 2

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 concerns financial
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Sigall v.
Taft (1962) 58 Cal 2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at actual impropriety,
but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980)
103Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a
financial interest. (People v Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies

2 Additionally, Regulation 18704 provides exceptions to when an official is making, participating in, or
influencing a governmental decision and provides that these do not include: "Actions by a public official relating to his
or her compensation or the terms or conditions of his or her employment or consulting contract. However, an official
may not make a decision to appoint, hire, fire, promote, demote, or suspend without pay or take disciplinary action with
financial sanction against the official or his or her immediate family, or set a salary for the official or his or her
immediate family different from salaries paid to other employees of the government agency in the same job
classification or position." SBCERA employees would be asked to participate in decisions that would affect the salary
of all employees in the same job classification of "Tier 1" employees, thus under the Act they would also not be
making, participating in or influencing a governmental decision.

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 5

regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. ( Id. at pp. 646-
649.) The result of a contract that is void based on a Section 1090 violation is that the party that
contracted with the public entity must disgorge any profit that resulted from the contract and the
public entity keeps the benefit of the contract. (See Carson Redevelopment Agency v.
Padilla (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323.)

Step One-Three: Is there a public officer who is subject to Section 1090 participating in
contract decisions?

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

County employees are public officers subject to Section 1090, therefore SBCERA
employees are subject to Section 1090. The applicability of Section 1090 also depends upon
whether there is a contract involved. You have stated that yourself and the SBCERA Tier 1
employees all have employment contracts with SBCERA and are not part of a collective bargaining
unit, therefore there is a contract involved.³ Yourself or other SBCERA Tier 1 employees may be
asked to participate in an upcoming Board decision that would alter SBCERA Tier 1 employee
employment contracts, by providing documents or analysis to the Board.

Steps Four and Five: Do you and other similarly situated SBCERA employees have a
financial interest in the contract and are there any applicable exceptions?

Under Section 1090, officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they
might profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Although Section 1090 does
not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state
that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses,
or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. Vallerga (1977) 67
Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158,
161-162 (2001).)

The Attorney General has recognized that an employee of a government agency has a
financial interest in receiving a salary as an employee. (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 274; 97
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.62.) In a 2014 opinion, the Attorney General advised that a trustee of a
community college district who was also a retired community college president and receiving the
same health benefits as current community college employees under his retirement plan from the
community college, was prohibited under Section 1090 from renegotiating current faculty health
benefits because "such a financial interest in the amount of the health benefits subject to
renegotiation comes within the general language of Section 1090." (97 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.62
(2014).) The Attorney General found no exception to Section 1090 that would permit the
community college trustee to renegotiate the health benefits of the district employees stating
"Neither case law nor our own opinions have extended these exceptions to include circumstances
where the public official 'has a personal financial interest . . . in the terms of a contract between the

³ The Attorney General previously concluded that public employment constitutes a "contract" within the
meaning of Section 1090. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 308, fn. 4 (1962); 36 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 121 (1960).)

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 6

governing body and its own employees' (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., 217, 221 (2006). "To do so would,
in effect, condone an 'obvious conflict'…"

In its decision in Lexin v. Superior Court, the California Supreme Court expressly endorsed
the Attorney General's conclusion on that point:

"[T]he Attorney General considered [in 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 217
(2006)] whether a community college district board member could participate in
collective bargaining negotiations when his own personal health benefits, as a
retired faculty member, were directly tied to those of the faculty with whom the
district board would be negotiating. The Attorney General correctly concluded
that, notwithstanding section 1091, subdivision (b)(13) and section 1091.5(a)(9),
the board member could not. [Citation.] While the retirement health benefits
qualified as government salary for purposes of the two provisions, the contract
nevertheless created a personal financial interest--the board member's health
benefits would rise or fall according to the results of the negotiations. The board
member thus faced a 'two masters' problem: as a board member he was obligated
to conserve the district's resources, while personally he stood to benefit if the
board was lavish in increasing faculty benefits.

…

[The government salary exception] is a defense if one's financial interest in a
proposed contract is only the present interest in an existing employment
relationship with a first or second party to the proposed contract, and thus an
interest in whatever indirect or incidental benefits might arise from the simple fact
of contracting with or on behalf of one's employer. It does not extend further to
contracts that . . . most directly affect one's interests by actually altering the terms
of one's employment; such interests directly implicate the 'two masters' problem
section 1090 was designed to eliminate." (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47
Cal.4th 1050.)"

The SBCERA Board decision involving the pick-up is similar to the decision discussed in
the Attorney General Opinion regarding the community college trustee in that SBCERA employees
would be asked to participate in decisions to alter the terms of their employment, specifically their
salary. This is the same "obvious conflict" recognized by the Attorney General and the Court in
Lexin.

Therefore, there is a conflict under Section 1090 if SBCERA employees participate in the
Board decision to eliminate the pick-up in their official capacities, however, they would not be
prohibited from participating in their individual capacities as discussed below.

In Campagna v. City of Sanger (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 533, a question arose in the case of a
contract city attorney negotiating an agreement with an outside attorney for litigation services on
behalf of the city. The city had an existing agreement with the city attorney's firm to provide legal
services that did not include litigation services. A dispute arose between the city and a number of

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 7

chemical companies and litigation services were needed. The city attorney negotiated a contract
with an outside firm for litigation services on behalf of the city. The contract contemplated that the
city attorney's firm and the outside firm would litigate the cases on contingency. The city then
complained the city attorney violated Section 1090 when negotiating the contract because the
attorney negotiated a referral fee for his firm in his official capacity as city attorney as part of the
agreement rather than a contingency fee, thus giving him a prohibited financial interest in the
contract. The court noted that it would be acceptable and not in violation of Section 1090 for the
city attorney to contract with the city for additional services beyond his original services agreement
should they be needed. However, the court found that it was not acceptable for an attorney to
negotiate a "referral fee" for his firm when negotiating on behalf of the city in his official capacity
with another law firm to perform legal services for the city.

Citing Campagna, the Attorney General acknowledged "that--at the time a contract city
attorney negotiates an initial services contract with the city--he or she is not acting as the city
attorney but rather as a private attorney engaged in an arm's-length transaction with a prospective
public agency client." The Attorney General further noted that "assuming the attorney does not
already serve the city in some other relevant capacity (e.g., city council member), section 1090 has
no application to the making of the original services contract, whatever the terms might be. Further,
during the term of the original services contract, whatever the terms might be, the contract attorney
may negotiate and agree with the city to perform and be compensated for services beyond his or her previously agreed-upon
duties." (99 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 35 (2016).)

The court in Campagna and the Attorney General recognized that in the case of an official
that is not a board member or city councilmember, one can participate in contract negotiations with a
government entity for compensation for services even if there already is an existing contract for
services. In these cases, the official acts on his or own individual capacity not his or her "official
capacity." Applying this line of reasoning to the SBCERA Tier 1 employees, they would be acting
in their official capacities should they provide the information to the Board in their official
capacities such as an official analysis on the pick-up decision and therefore would have a conflict of
interest under Section 1090 because acting in this manner is not negotiating on their own behalf, but
rather advising in an official capacity where they are prohibited by the "two masters" problem.
However, should SBCERA employees seek to contact the Board on their own behalf on the pick-up
issue and make it clear that they are appearing in their individual capacities they would not be
prohibited by Section 1090 from doing so.

Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply?

In non-procurement situations, the rule of necessity has permitted a public officer to carry
out the essential duties of the office despite a conflict of interest where the officer is the only one
who may legally act. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).) In non-procurement situations, the
rule of necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of
interest exists. (Ibid.)

A situation may arise where affected SBCERA employees are the only ones who may
legally act to carry out an essential duty of their offices. If you need further assistance determining
if the rule of necessity applies to any particular officer or employee, you should seek further

File No. A-17-087
Page No. 8

assistance identifying the official and the reasons it is believed the official is only one that can
legally act. However, if it is possible for an outside party or unaffected employee to provide the
necessary documentation or analysis these tasks must be performed by someone other than an
affected SBCERA employee.

Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section 1090 prohibits SBCERA employees
that receive the pick-up from participating in their official capacities in the decisions you have
described. However, under the rule of necessity, an affected employee may participate by providing
necessary documentation or analysis to the Board only if there is no other method of providing such
documentation or analysis to the Board. Additionally, SBCERA employees may appear at
SBCERA Board meetings or have contact with Board members related to the pick-up issue if it is
made clear that they are speaking in their individual capacities and not their official capacities.⁴

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature: Sukhi K. Brar]

By:    Sukhi K. Brar
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl




⁴ Section 1090 would not require the SBCERA employees to leave the room during the discussion of this item.

sections.question:
Do SBCERA staff have a conflict of interest in participating in SBCERA Board decisions 
related to eliminating a pick-up benefit in their employment contracts either in their official or 
individual capacities?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, SBCERA staff that receive the pick-up benefit have a conflict of interest in 
participating in SBCERA Board decisions to eliminate the benefit in their official capacities under 
Section I 090, though the rule of necessity may allow limited participation in some circumstances. 
,However, the employees are not prohibited from participating in their individual capacities as 
explained below.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 820 48 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state 
or local government agency, including city councilmembers. 
Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103 
and the financial interests implicated by your facts are the following:  
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his 
or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a l 0-percent or greater interest. 
(Section 82030(a).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
The Act excludes from the definition of"income" any "salary and reimbursement for 
expenses or per diem, and social security, or other similar benefit payments received from a state, 
local, or federal government agency . . .. " (Section 82030(b)(2).) The salary paid by a state, local or 
federal government agency are generally not considered "income" under Section 82030(b)(2) and 
Regulation 18232(a). Therefore, the SBCERA employees will not have an interest in the Board as a 
source of income. However, material financial effects on an official's government salary may still 
be disqualifying under limited circumstances as a material and foreseeable financial effect on the 
official's personal finances that is distinct from other officials. (See Boydston Advice Letter, No. A-
12-133.) Thus, we continue the analysis based on financial effects to SBCERA employees' personal 
finances. 
Foreseeability 
A public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision, if the 
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on one or more of his or 
her financial interests. (Section 87103.) 
Regulation 18701 provides in pertinent part: 
"(a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on 
a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if 
the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the 
subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract 
with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a 
real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) - (6)." 
SBCERA employees may be asked to advise the Board on upcoming decisions involving 
the elimination of a pick-up that is a supplement to SBCERA employees' income. Therefore, 
SBCERA employee income is the subject of the decision before the SBC ERA Board. Therefore, 
SBCERA employees' personal finances are the subject of this governmental decision. 


Materiality  
Regulation 18702.5 provides the appropriate materiality standard for personal financial 
effects: 
"(a) A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental 
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate 
family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate 
family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the 
decision. 
(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a), a personal financial effect does not 
include: 
( 1) Any establishment of or change to benefits provided under an employment 
or retirement policy for employees or retirees if the financial effect of the decision 
applies equally to all employees in the same bargaining unit or other 
representative group . . .. " 
SBCERA's Tier 1 employees will receive a measurable loss if the pick-up is eliminated. 
However, this loss would apply equally to all employees who currently receive or are entitled to 
receive the pick-up in the future per their employment contracts, therefore the financial effect upon 
these employees' personal finances is not material pursuant to Regulation 18702.S(b)(I). 2 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 concerns financial 
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at actual impropriety, 
but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 
103Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a 
financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson , •. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
: Additionally, Regulation 18704 provides exceptions to when an official is making, participating in, or 
influencing a governmental decision and provides that these do not include: "Actions by a public official relating to his 
or her compensation or the terms or conditions of his or her employment or consulting contract. However, an official 
may not make a decision to appoint, hire, fire, promote, demote, or suspend without pay or take disciplinary action with 
financial sanction against the official or his or her immediate family, or set a salary for the official or his or her 
immediate family different from salaries paid to other employees of the government agency in the same job 
classification or position." SBCERA employees would be asked to participate in decisions that would affect the salary 
of all employees in the same job classification of "Tier I" employees, thus under the Act they would also not be 
making, participating in or influencing a governmental decision.  
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. ( Id. at pp. 646-
649.) The result of a contract that is void based on a Section I 090 violation is that the party that 
contracted with the public entity must disgorge any profit that resulted from the contract and the 
public entity keeps the benefit of the contract. (See Carson Redevelopment Agency v. 
Padilla (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323.) 
Step One-Three: Is there a public officer who is subject to Section 1090 participating in 
contract decisions? 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
County employees are public officers subject to Section 1090, therefore SBCERA 
employees are subject to Section I 090. The applicability of Section I 090 also depends upon 
whether there is a contract involved. You have stated that yourself and the SBCERA Tier 1 
employees all have employment contracts with SBCERA and are not part of a collective bargaining 
unit, therefore there is a contract involved. 3 Yourself or other SB CERA Tier I employees may be 
asked to participate in an upcoming Board decision that would alter SBCERA Tier I employee 
employment contracts, by providing documents or analysis to the Board. 
Steps Four and Five: Do you and other similarly situated SBCERA employees have a 
financial interest in the contract and are there any applicable exceptions? 
Under Section I 090, officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they 
might profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Although Section 1090 does 
not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state

or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. Vallerga ( 1977) 67

161-162 (2001).) 
The Attorney General has recognized that an employee of a government agency has a 
financial interest in receiving a salary as an employee. (81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 274; 97 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.62.) In a 2014 opinion, the Attorney General advised that a trustee of a 
community college district who was also a retired community college president and receiving the 
same health benefits as current community college employees under his retirement plan from the 
community college, was prohibited under Section 1090 from renegotiating current faculty health 
benefits because "such a financial interest in the amount of the health benefits subject to 
renegotiation comes within the general language of Section 1090." (97 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.62 
(2014).) The Attorney General found no exception to Section 1090 that would permit the 
community college trustee to renegotiate the health benefits of the district employees stating 
"Neither case law nor our own opinions have extended these exceptions to include circumstances 
where the public official 'has a personal financial interest . . .  in the terms of a contract between the 
1 The Attorney General previously concluded that public employment constitutes a "contract" within the 
meaning of Section I 090. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 308. fu. 4( 1962); 36 Ops .. Cal.Atty.Gen. 121 ( 1960).)  
governing body and its own employees' (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., 217, 221 (2006). "To do so would, 
in effect, condone an 'obvious conflict' ... " 
In its decision in Lexin v. Superior Court, the California Supreme Court expressly endorsed 
the Attorney General's conclusion on that point: 
"[T]he Attorney General considered [in 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 217 
(2006)] whether a community college district board member could participate in 
collective bargaining negotiations when his own personal health benefits, as a 
retired faculty member, were directly tied to those of the faculty with whom the 
district board would be negotiating. The Attorney General correctly concluded 
that, notwithstanding section 1091, subdivision (b)(l3) and section 1091.5(a)(9), 
the board member could not. [Citation.] While the retirement health benefits 
qualified as government salary for purposes of the two provisions, the contract 
nevertheless created a personal financial interest--the board member's health 
benefits would rise or fall according to the results of the negotiations. The board 
member thus faced a 'two masters' problem: as a board member he was obligated 
to conserve the district's resources, while personally he stood to benefit if the 
board was lavish in increasing faculty benefits. 
[The government salary exception] is a defense if one's financial interest in a 
proposed contract is only the present interest in an existing employment 
relationship with a first or second party to the proposed contract, and thus an 
interest in whatever indirect or incidental benefits might arise from the simple fact 
of contracting with or on behalf of one's employer. It does not extend further to 
contracts that . . .  most directly affect one's interests by actually altering the terms 
of one's employment; such interests directly implicate the 'two masters' problems 
section 1090 was designed to eliminate." (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 
Cal.4th l 050. )" 
The SBCERA Board decision involving the pick-up is similar to the decision discussed in 
the Attorney General Opinion regarding the community college trustee in that SBCERA employees 
would be asked to participate in decisions to alter the terms of their employment, specifically their 
salary. This is the same "obvious conflict" recognized by the Attorney General and the Court in 
Lexin. 
Therefore, there is a conflict under Section 1090 if SBCERA employees participate in the 
Board decision to eliminate the pick-up in their official capacities, however, they would not be 
prohibited from participating in their individual capacities as discussed below. 
In Campagna v. City of Sanger (1996) 42 Cal.App. 4th 533, a question arose in the case of a 
contract city attorney negotiating an agreement with an outside attorney for litigation services on 
behalf of the city. The city had an existing agreement with the city attorney's firm to provide legal 
services that did not include litigation services. A dispute arose between the city and a number of  
chemical companies and litigation services were needed. The city attorney negotiated a contract 
with an outside firm for litigation services on behalf of the city. The contract contemplated that the 
city attorney's firm and the outside firm would litigate the cases on contingency. The city then 
complained the city attorney violated Section 1090 when negotiating the contract because the 
attorney negotiated a referral fee for his firm in his official capacity as city attorney as part of the 
agreement rather than a contingency fee, thus giving him a prohibited financial interest in the 
contract. The court noted that it would be acceptable and not in violation of Section 1090 for the 
city attorney to contract with the city for additional services beyond his original services agreement 
should they be needed. However, the court found that it was not acceptable for an attorney to 
negotiate a "referral fee" for his firm when negotiating on behalf of the city in his official capacity 
with another law firm to perform legal services for the city. 
Citing Campagna, the Attorney General acknowledged "that--at the time a contract city 
attorney negotiates an initial services contract with the city--he or she is not acting as the city 
attorney but rather as a private attorney engaged in an arm's-length transaction with a prospective 
public agency client." The Attorney General further noted that "assuming the attorney does not 
already serve the city in some other relevant capacity ( e.g., city council member), section 1090 has 
no application to the making of the original services contract, whatever the terms might be. Further, 
during the term of the original services contract, the contract attorney may negotiate and agree with 
the city to perform and be compensated for services beyond his or her previously agreed-upon 
duties." (99 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 35 (2016).) 
The court in Campagna and the Attorney General recognized that in the case of an official 
that is not a board member or city councilmember, one can participate in contact negotiations with a 
government entity for compensation for services even if there already is an existing contract for 
services. In these cases, the official acts on his or own individual capacity not his or her "official 
capacity." Applying this line ofreasoning to the SBCERA Tier I employees, they would be acting 
in their official capacities should they provide the information to the Board in their official 
capacities such as an official analysis on the pick-up decision and therefore would have a conflict of 
interest under Section l 090 because acting in this manner is not negotiating on their own behalf, but 
rather advising in an official capacity where they are prohibited by the "two masters" problem. 
However, should SBCERA employees seek to contact the Board on their own behalf on the pick-up 
issue and make it clear that they are appearing in their individual capacities they would not be 
prohibited by Section I 090 from doing so. 
Step Six: Docs the rule of necessity apply? 
In non-procurement situations, the rule of necessity has permitted a public officer to carry 
out the essential duties of the office despite a conflict of interest where the officer is the only one 
who may legally act. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).) In non-procurement situations, the 
rule of necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of 
interest exists. (Ibid.) 
A situation may arise where affected SBCERA employees are the only ones who may 
legally act to carry out an essential duty of their offices. If you need further assistance determining 
if the_ rule of necessity applies to any particular officer or employee, you should seek further  
assistance identifying the official and the reasons it is believed the official is only one that can 
legally act. However, if it is possible for an outside party or unaffected employee to provide the 
necessary documentation or analysis these tasks must be performed by someone other than an 
affected SBCERA employee. 
Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section I 090 prohibits SB CERA employees 
that receive the pick-up from participating in their official capacities in the decisions you have 
described. However, under the rule of necessity, an affected employee may participate by providing 
necessary documentation or analysis to the Board only ifthere is no other method of providing such 
documentation or analysis to the Board. Additionally, SBCERA employees may appear at 
SBCERA Board meetings or have contact with Board members related to the pick-up issue if it is 
made clear that they are speaking in their individual capacities and not their official capacities.4

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do SBCERA staff have a conflict of interest in participating in SBCERA Board decisions 
related to eliminating a pick-up benefit in their employment contracts either in their official or 
individual capacities?

CONCLUSION: Yes, SBCERA staff that receive the pick-up benefit have a conflict of interest in 
participating in SBCERA Board decisions to eliminate the benefit in their official capacities under 
Section I 090, though the rule of necessity may allow limited participation in some circumstances. 
,However, the employees are not prohibited from participating in their individual capacities as 
explained below.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 12, 2017 Michael Calabrese Chief Counsel San Bernandino CERA 348 W. Hospitality Lane San Bernardino, CA 92415 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-087 Dear Mr. Calabrese: You are the Chief Counsel of the San Bernardino County Employees' Retirement Association (SBCERA) and this letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Additionally, the Commission does not advise about past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).) Therefore, nothing in this advice letter should be construed to advise about any conduct that has already taken place. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Bernardino County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTION Do SBCERA staff have a conflict of interest in participating in SBCERA Board decisions related to eliminating a pick-up benefit in their employment contracts either in their official or individual capacities? CONCLUSION Yes, SBCERA staff that receive the pick-up benefit have a conflict of interest in participating in SBCERA Board decisions to eliminate the benefit in their official capacities under Section 1090, though the rule of necessity may allow limited participation in some circumstances. ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-087 Page No. 2 However, the employees are not prohibited from participating in their individual capacities as explained below. FACTS SBCERA is a retirement system formed under the County Employees' Retirement Law of 1937. You are the Chief Counsel of SBCERA and are writing for advice on your own behalf and for several dozen similarly situated employees of SBCERA. Many of SBCERA's employees currently receive, as part of their compensation, a "pick-up" by SBCERA of the retirement contributions that the employees would otherwise be required to make toward the funding of their pensions. This pick-up
